1:
"Be ye therefore followers of God, as dear children;" The witnesses note the call to imitate God as beloved children. On "be ye therefore followers of God," the grammatical voices note the imitation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's lofty exhortation (drawing the consequence "therefore" from 4:32): "be ye followers (mimetai — imitators) of God" — believers are to imitate God himself, especially in his forgiving love and goodness.

On "as dear children," the witnesses note the motive. Barnes and Henry note the ground: "as dear (beloved) children" — as children naturally imitate a loved father, so believers, God's beloved children, are to copy their Father; the relationship makes the imitation fitting.

The Reformed Calvin notes the height: nothing less than the imitation of God himself is set before the believer; the dignity of being his children calls for likeness to him. The verse displays the call to imitate God as beloved children.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul exhorts "be ye followers (imitators) of God," believers imitating God himself, especially in forgiving love and goodness (Barnes, Robertson); "as dear (beloved) children," as children copy a loved father, God's beloved children copying their Father (Barnes, Henry). Calvin notes the imitation of God himself is set before the believer. The verse displays imitating God. The verse marks the call: followers of God. The verse displays the motive: as dear children. The verse displays the likeness: to the Father. The verse displays the dignity: God's children. The call displays believers copying their beloved Father. The verse displays the imitation: of God. The verse displays the children: beloved. The verse displays the likeness: filial. The call to imitate God as beloved children displays the high standard of the Christian life: as children naturally copy a loved father, so believers, being God's dear children, are to imitate God himself — especially in his forgiving love. The verse displays the imitation of God, defined as walking in Christ's self-giving love (v.2). The astonishing height of the command is itself worth pausing over, for the apostle does not merely set before believers some good man or holy pattern to copy, but God himself, and chiefly his readiness to forgive and his overflowing goodness; and the warrant for so lofty an aim lies in the relationship, since those who have been adopted as the beloved children of God are thereby summoned to bear the family likeness of their Father.
2:
"And walk in love, as Christ also hath loved us, and hath given himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God for a sweetsmelling savour." The witnesses note the call to walk in love after the pattern of Christ's self-sacrifice. On "walk in love, as Christ also hath loved us," the grammatical voices note the pattern. Barnes and Robertson note the command: "walk (order your whole life) in love, as (after the pattern of) Christ also hath loved us" — Christ's love is the model and measure of the believer's love.

On "hath given himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God," the witnesses note the atonement. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ's love was shown in that he "gave himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God" — his self-surrender on the cross, a true sacrifice (the terms drawn from the Levitical offerings) presented to God on our behalf.

On "for a sweetsmelling savour," the witnesses note the acceptance. Barnes and Calvin note the figure (Leviticus): Christ's sacrifice was "a sweetsmelling savour" — a fragrant offering, well-pleasing and acceptable to God, who was satisfied by it. The verse displays the call to walk in love after Christ's self-sacrifice.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "walk in love, as (after the pattern of) Christ also hath loved us," Christ's love the model of the believer's (Barnes, Robertson); his love shown in that he "gave himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God," his self-surrender on the cross a true sacrifice (Barnes, Calvin); "a sweetsmelling savour," a fragrant offering well-pleasing to God (Barnes, Calvin). The verse displays walking in love. The verse marks the command: walk in love. The verse displays the pattern: Christ's love. The verse displays the sacrifice: he gave himself. The verse displays the acceptance: a sweet savour. The call displays love modelled on Christ's self-giving. The verse displays the love: Christ's. The verse displays the offering: himself. The verse displays the fragrance: acceptable. The call to walk in love after the pattern of Christ's self-sacrifice displays the measure of Christian love: believers are to order their whole life in love, after the pattern of Christ, who loved us and gave himself for us — a true sacrifice to God, a fragrant offering well-pleasing to him. The verse displays the self-giving love of Christ, against which all impurity is set (vv.3-5).
3:
"But fornication, and all uncleanness, or covetousness, let it not be once named among you, as becometh saints;" The brief verse notes the call to utter purity, that sin be not even named among saints. The grammatical voices note the prohibition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, turning from love to its opposite, forbids "fornication, and all uncleanness (impurity of every kind), or covetousness (greed, here often linked with impurity)" — the sins of lust and grasping desire.

On "let it not be once named among you," the witnesses note the strictness. Barnes and Henry note the high standard: these sins are not to "be once named (mentioned with approval, or spoken of lightly) among you" — so far should believers be from them that they should not even be a topic of casual or approving talk.

On "as becometh saints," the Reformed Calvin notes the fitness: such purity "becometh (is fitting for) saints" — the holy ones, set apart to God; their character demands the utter avoidance of these sins. The verse displays the call to utter purity.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul forbids "fornication, and all uncleanness, or covetousness," the sins of lust and grasping desire (Barnes, Robertson); these not to "be once named (mentioned with approval, spoken of lightly) among you," so far should believers be from them (Barnes, Henry); "as becometh saints," such purity fitting for the holy ones set apart to God (Calvin). The verse displays utter purity. The verse marks the prohibition: not fornication or uncleanness. The verse displays the strictness: not once named. The verse displays the standard: high. The verse displays the fitness: as becometh saints. The call displays believers far removed from impurity. The verse displays the sins: forbidden. The verse displays the naming: not even. The verse displays the saints: pure. The call to utter purity, that sin be not even named among saints, displays the high holiness required: fornication, all uncleanness, and covetousness are not so much as to be spoken of lightly among believers — for such purity befits the saints, the holy ones set apart to God. The verse displays the demand for purity, with a warning against corrupt speech (v.4).
4:
"Neither filthiness, nor foolish talking, nor jesting, which are not convenient: but rather giving of thanks." The brief verse notes the call to pure speech, replacing corrupt talk with thanksgiving. The grammatical voices note the sins of the tongue. Barnes and Robertson note Paul forbids three sins of speech: "filthiness (obscenity, shameful talk), foolish talking (silly, empty speech), nor jesting (coarse wit, ribald joking)" — "which are not convenient (not fitting, unbecoming)" for believers.

On "jesting," the witnesses note the nuance. Barnes and Ellicott note that "jesting" (eutrapelia) means not innocent cheerfulness but the clever, coarse wit that turns easily to indecency; it is the abuse of humor, not all pleasantry, that is condemned.

On "but rather giving of thanks," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the substitute: in place of corrupt or empty talk, believers are to give "thanks" — the speech that becomes saints is gracious and thankful, not filthy or foolish. The verse displays the call to pure speech, replacing corrupt talk with thanksgiving.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul forbids "filthiness (obscenity), foolish talking (empty speech), nor jesting (coarse wit)," "not convenient (unbecoming)" for believers (Barnes, Robertson); "jesting" being not innocent cheer but the clever wit that turns to indecency, the abuse of humor (Barnes, Ellicott); "but rather giving of thanks," gracious thankful speech replacing corrupt talk (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays pure speech. The verse marks the sins: filthiness, foolish talk, jesting. The verse displays the unfitness: not convenient. The verse displays the nuance: coarse wit. The verse displays the substitute: thanksgiving. The call displays corrupt talk replaced by thankful speech. The verse displays the filthiness: forbidden. The verse displays the jesting: coarse. The verse displays the thanks: instead. The call to pure speech, replacing corrupt talk with thanksgiving, displays the sanctifying of the tongue: obscenity, empty talk, and coarse jesting are unbecoming in believers — in their place is to come gracious thanksgiving, the speech that befits the saints. The verse displays the purity of speech, backed by the warning of judgment (v.5). The principle reaches further than the particular sins named, for it lays down that the tongue of the believer, redeemed along with the rest of him, is to be put to a use worthy of its Maker; and so over against every form of corrupt, empty, or coarse speech the apostle sets the single wholesome alternative of thanksgiving, as though to say that the mouth which might have uttered filthiness is far better employed in blessing God.
5:
"For this ye know, that no whoremonger, nor unclean person, nor covetous man, who is an idolater, hath any inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God." The witnesses note the warning that the impure and covetous have no inheritance in the kingdom. On "no whoremonger, nor unclean person, nor covetous man," the grammatical voices note the excluded. Barnes and Robertson note the solemn certainty ("this ye know"): "no whoremonger (fornicator), nor unclean person, nor covetous man" — those habitually given to impurity or greed are shut out.

On "who is an idolater," the witnesses note the equation. Barnes and Chrysostom note the striking identification: the covetous man "is an idolater" — covetousness is idolatry, for the greedy man makes a god of his wealth and desire, worshipping the creature instead of the Creator (Colossians 3:5).

On "hath any inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God," the Reformed Calvin notes the exclusion: such persons "have no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God" — the habitual practice of these sins bars from the kingdom; a sobering warning. The verse displays the warning that the impure and covetous have no inheritance.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the solemn certainty — "no whoremonger, nor unclean person, nor covetous man," those given to impurity or greed shut out (Barnes, Robertson); the covetous man "is an idolater," covetousness being idolatry, the greedy making a god of wealth (Barnes, Chrysostom); such have "no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God," the habitual practice barring from the kingdom (Calvin). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the excluded: the impure and covetous. The verse displays the equation: covetousness is idolatry. The verse displays the exclusion: no inheritance. The verse displays the certainty: this ye know. The warning displays the impure shut out of the kingdom. The verse displays the impure: excluded. The verse displays the idolater: the covetous. The verse displays the kingdom: forfeited. The warning that the impure and covetous have no inheritance in the kingdom displays the gravity behind the call to purity: those habitually given to fornication, uncleanness, or covetousness — which is idolatry — have no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God. The verse displays the exclusion of the impure, against the deceivers who would excuse sin (v.6).
6:
"Let no man deceive you with vain words: for because of these things cometh the wrath of God upon the children of disobedience." The brief verse notes the warning against deceivers and the wrath of God on the disobedient. The grammatical voices note the caution. Barnes and Robertson note Paul warns: "let no man deceive you with vain (empty, hollow) words" — against those who would excuse or make light of these sins with plausible but empty arguments.

On "because of these things cometh the wrath of God," the witnesses note the certainty of judgment. Barnes and Calvin note the ground: "because of these things (the sins of impurity and covetousness) cometh the wrath of God" — God's wrath surely falls on such sins; they are not trivial but bring down judgment.

On "upon the children of disobedience," the grammatical-historical note the objects: the wrath comes "upon the children of disobedience" — those characterized by disobedience to God (the same phrase as 2:2); the impenitent who practise these sins. The verse displays the warning against deceivers and the wrath of God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul warns "let no man deceive you with vain (empty) words," against those who excuse these sins with plausible arguments (Barnes, Robertson); "because of these things cometh the wrath of God," God's wrath surely falling on such sins, not trivial but judgment-bringing (Barnes, Calvin); "upon the children of disobedience," those marked by disobedience to God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the warning of wrath. The verse marks the caution: be not deceived. The verse displays the deceit: vain words. The verse displays the judgment: the wrath of God. The verse displays the objects: the disobedient. The warning displays God's wrath falling on impurity. The verse displays the deceivers: warned against. The verse displays the wrath: coming. The verse displays the disobedient: judged. The warning against deceivers and the wrath of God on the disobedient displays the seriousness of the matter: believers are not to be deceived by empty words that excuse impurity and greed, for because of these very sins the wrath of God comes upon the children of disobedience. The verse displays the certainty of judgment, calling believers to separation (v.7).
7:
"Be not ye therefore partakers with them." The brief verse notes the call not to share in the sins of the disobedient. The grammatical voices note the separation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's conclusion ("therefore"): "be not ye partakers (sharers, fellow-participants) with them" — believers are not to join in the sins of the children of disobedience, nor share in their conduct.

On "with them," the witnesses note the company avoided. Barnes and Gill note that to be "partakers with them" would be to take part in their sins and so in their judgment; believers are to keep clear of both the deeds and the doom of the disobedient.

The grammatical-historical note the force: the warning of wrath (v.6) grounds this command — since wrath falls on these sins, the believer must not partake in them. The verse displays the call not to share in the sins of the disobedient.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul concludes "be not ye partakers (sharers) with them," not joining in the sins of the children of disobedience (Barnes, Robertson); to partake with them would be to share their sins and judgment, so believers keep clear of both deeds and doom (Barnes, Gill). The grammatical-historical note the warning of wrath grounds the command. The verse displays the call to separation. The verse marks the command: be not partakers. The verse displays the company: the disobedient. The verse displays the danger: sharing their sin and doom. The verse displays the ground: the wrath that comes. The call displays believers keeping clear of the disobedient's sins. The verse displays the partaking: forbidden. The verse displays the separation: required. The verse displays the doom: avoided. The call not to share in the sins of the disobedient displays the believer's duty of separation: since the wrath of God falls on these sins, believers are not to be partakers with the children of disobedience — sharing neither their deeds nor their doom. The verse displays the call to separation, grounded in the believer's new nature as light (v.8). The command is no mere counsel of prudence but a necessary inference from the warning just given, for if the wrath of God is even now coming upon the children of disobedience because of these very sins, then to share in the sins would be to share in the sentence; and therefore the apostle would have his readers stand wholly clear, neither joining in the deeds of darkness nor exposing themselves to the judgment those deeds inevitably draw down.
8:
"For ye were sometimes darkness, but now are ye light in the Lord: walk as children of light:" The witnesses note the contrast of former darkness and present light, and the call to walk as light. On "ye were sometimes darkness, but now are ye light in the Lord," the grammatical voices note the transformation. Barnes and Robertson note the strong figure: not merely in darkness and in light, but believers "were (themselves) darkness" (so given over to ignorance and sin) and "now are light in the Lord" — themselves become light, illumined and transformed in union with the Lord.

On "light in the Lord," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Calvin note that the change is "in the Lord" — believers are light not of themselves but by union with Christ, the light of the world; their illumination flows from him.

On "walk as children of light," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the duty: being now light, they are to "walk as children of light" — to live consistently with their new nature, in holiness and truth; the new being requires a new walk. The verse displays the contrast of former darkness and present light.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the strong figure — believers "were darkness" (given over to sin) and "now are light in the Lord," themselves become light, transformed in union with the Lord (Barnes, Robertson); the change "in the Lord," light not of themselves but by union with Christ (Barnes, Calvin); "walk as children of light," living consistently with the new nature (Wesley). The verse displays darkness to light. The verse marks the past: ye were darkness. The verse displays the present: now light in the Lord. The verse displays the source: in the Lord. The verse displays the duty: walk as light. The contrast displays believers transformed from darkness to light. The verse displays the darkness: former. The verse displays the light: present. The verse displays the walk: as children of light. The contrast of former darkness and present light, and the call to walk as light, display the transformation grace works: believers were once darkness itself, but now are light in the Lord — and being light, they are to walk as children of light, in lives that match their new nature. The verse displays the walk of light, whose fruit is goodness and truth (v.9).
9:
"(For the fruit of the Spirit is in all goodness and righteousness and truth;)" The brief verse notes the fruit of the light: goodness, righteousness, and truth. The grammatical voices note the parenthesis. Barnes and Robertson note the parenthetical explanation of what the walk of light produces: "the fruit of the Spirit (or, of the light, as some manuscripts read) is in all goodness and righteousness and truth" — the threefold fruit that the light yields.

On the three graces, the witnesses note the cluster. Barnes and Calvin note the fruit: "goodness (benevolence, kindness toward others), righteousness (justice, uprightness), and truth (sincerity, integrity, honesty)" — the moral fruit that marks the children of light.

The grammatical-historical note the contrast: this fruit of light stands over against the unfruitful works of darkness (v.11); the light is fruitful in every good thing, darkness barren. The verse displays the fruit of the light.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the parenthetical explanation — "the fruit of the Spirit (or light) is in all goodness and righteousness and truth," the threefold fruit the light yields (Barnes, Robertson); the three being "goodness (kindness), righteousness (uprightness), and truth (sincerity)," the moral fruit of the children of light (Barnes, Calvin); standing over against the unfruitful works of darkness (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fruit of light. The verse marks the fruit: of the light. The verse displays the graces: goodness, righteousness, truth. The verse displays the contrast: with darkness. The verse displays the mark: of the children of light. The fruit displays the light yielding every good thing. The verse displays the goodness: kindness. The verse displays the righteousness: uprightness. The verse displays the truth: sincerity. The fruit of the light — goodness, righteousness, and truth — displays what the walk of light produces: the children of light bear the threefold fruit of goodness toward others, righteousness in conduct, and truth in sincerity — over against the barren works of darkness. The verse displays the fruit of light, to be proved as pleasing to the Lord (v.10). The reading found in the better manuscripts, fruit of the light rather than of the Spirit, fits the context the more aptly, for the apostle is contrasting the barren and shameful deeds of darkness with the rich harvest that the light produces wherever it shines; and the threefold cluster he names, goodness toward men, righteousness in dealing, and truth in word, marks out the whole compass of a holy life as the natural yield of those who have themselves become light in the Lord.
10:
"Proving what is acceptable unto the Lord." The brief verse notes the call to prove what is pleasing to the Lord. The grammatical voices note the discernment. Barnes and Robertson note that the children of light, in their walk, are "proving (dokimazo — testing, examining, discerning) what is acceptable (well-pleasing) unto the Lord" — making it their constant study to find out and do what pleases God.

On "acceptable unto the Lord," the witnesses note the standard. Barnes and JFB note that the measure of conduct is not human opinion or custom but "what is acceptable unto the Lord" — the believer's aim is to please Christ, testing every action by that standard.

The grammatical-historical note the activity: the walk of light is not passive but discerning — actively examining and approving what pleases God; holiness involves a thoughtful, proving mind. The verse displays the call to prove what is pleasing to the Lord.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the children of light are "proving (testing, discerning) what is acceptable (well-pleasing) unto the Lord," making it their study to find and do what pleases God (Barnes, Robertson); the standard being "what is acceptable unto the Lord," not human opinion but pleasing Christ (Barnes, JFB); the walk of light being active and discerning (grammatical-historical). The verse displays proving God's pleasure. The verse marks the activity: proving. The verse displays the object: what pleases the Lord. The verse displays the standard: acceptable to him. The verse displays the discernment: thoughtful. The call displays believers testing all by God's pleasure. The verse displays the proving: of conduct. The verse displays the pleasure: of the Lord. The verse displays the aim: to please Christ. The call to prove what is pleasing to the Lord displays the discerning mind of the walk of light: the children of light make it their constant study to test and approve what is acceptable to the Lord — measuring every action not by custom but by what pleases Christ. The verse displays the proving of God's will, set against fellowship with darkness (v.11). This proving is no idle or occasional exercise but the settled habit of the children of light, who carry with them through every circumstance a kind of inward touchstone by which they continually test what they are about to do; and the standard to which they bring it is neither their own inclination nor the custom of the world about them, but the single question of what will be found well-pleasing in the sight of the Lord whom they serve.
11:
"And have no fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness, but rather reprove them." The witnesses note the call to shun and reprove the works of darkness. On "have no fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness," the grammatical voices note the separation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's command: "have no fellowship (no participation, no sharing) with the unfruitful (barren, profitless) works of darkness" — the deeds of sin, which produce nothing good, are to be wholly avoided.

On "unfruitful works of darkness," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Calvin note the contrast with the fruit of light (v.9): the works of darkness are "unfruitful" — they yield no good fruit, only shame and ruin; barren in contrast to the fruitful light.

On "but rather reprove them," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the further duty: not only to avoid but to "reprove (elencho — expose, convict, rebuke) them" — believers are not merely to abstain from the works of darkness but to expose and rebuke them by their light and witness. The verse displays the call to shun and reprove the works of darkness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "have no fellowship (no participation) with the unfruitful (barren) works of darkness," the deeds of sin wholly avoided (Barnes, Robertson); the works "unfruitful," yielding no good fruit, only shame, in contrast to the fruit of light (Barnes, Calvin); "but rather reprove (expose, convict) them," not merely abstaining but exposing and rebuking them (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays shunning and reproving darkness. The verse marks the separation: no fellowship. The verse displays the works: unfruitful darkness. The verse displays the duty: reprove them. The verse displays the contrast: barren vs fruitful. The call displays believers shunning and exposing sin. The verse displays the fellowship: refused. The verse displays the works: barren. The verse displays the reproof: given. The call to shun and reprove the works of darkness displays the double duty of the children of light: they are to have no fellowship with the barren, profitless deeds of sin, and not merely to avoid them but to expose and rebuke them by their light. The verse displays the reproving of darkness, whose deeds are shameful (v.12).
12:
"For it is a shame even to speak of those things which are done of them in secret." The brief verse notes the shameful secret deeds of darkness. The grammatical voices note the reason. Barnes and Robertson note Paul gives a reason for shunning these works: "it is a shame (shameful, disgraceful) even to speak of those things which are done of them (the children of disobedience) in secret" — the deeds of darkness are so vile that merely to describe them is shameful.

On "done in secret," the witnesses note the concealment. Barnes and Calvin note that these sins are "done in secret" — hidden, practised in darkness and concealment, for they cannot bear the light; their secrecy is itself a mark of their shamefulness.

The grammatical-historical note the connection: this explains why believers reprove rather than partake — the deeds are so shameful that the pure cannot even speak of them without disgrace, much less share in them. The verse displays the shameful secret deeds of darkness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul gives the reason — "it is a shame (disgraceful) even to speak of those things which are done in secret," the deeds of darkness so vile that to describe them is shameful (Barnes, Robertson); "done in secret," hidden and practised in darkness, unable to bear the light, their secrecy a mark of shame (Barnes, Calvin); this explaining why believers reprove rather than partake (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the shameful deeds. The verse marks the shame: even to speak of them. The verse displays the secrecy: done in secret. The verse displays the vileness: cannot bear the light. The verse displays the connection: why reprove. The deeds display sin so shameful it shuns the light. The verse displays the shame: of the deeds. The verse displays the secret: their cover. The verse displays the disgrace: even to mention. The shameful secret deeds of darkness display why they must be shunned and reproved: the sins of the disobedient are so vile that it is a disgrace even to speak of them, practised as they are in secret because they cannot bear the light. The verse displays the shamefulness of darkness, exposed and transformed by the light (v.13).
13:
"But all things that are reproved are made manifest by the light: for whatsoever doth make manifest is light." The brief verse notes the light making manifest and exposing what is reproved. The grammatical voices note the exposing power. Barnes and Robertson note the principle: "all things that are reproved (exposed, convicted) are made manifest (brought to light, shown for what they are) by the light" — when the light shines on the works of darkness, it reveals their true nature.

On "whatsoever doth make manifest is light," the witnesses note the nature of light. Barnes and Calvin note the maxim: "whatsoever doth make manifest (reveal, bring to light) is light" — it is the very nature and property of light to reveal; that which exposes hidden things is itself light.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the application: the believer, as light, by his holy life and witness exposes the works of darkness for what they are; some add that the light not only exposes but may transform — what is made manifest by the light may itself become light. The verse displays the light making manifest and exposing.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the principle — "all things that are reproved (exposed) are made manifest (brought to light) by the light," the light revealing the true nature of the works of darkness (Barnes, Robertson); the maxim "whatsoever doth make manifest is light," it being light's nature to reveal (Barnes, Calvin); the believer as light exposing darkness, and the further thought that what is made manifest may become light (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the exposing light. The verse marks the exposing: made manifest by light. The verse displays the nature: light reveals. The verse displays the application: the believer as light. The verse displays the transformation: darkness made light. The light displays the exposing and transforming power of light. The verse displays the manifesting: of hidden deeds. The verse displays the light: its property. The verse displays the exposure: complete. The light making manifest and exposing what is reproved displays the power of the children of light: the light, by its very nature, reveals the works of darkness for what they are — and that which exposes is itself light, so that the believer's holy witness both unmasks and may transform. The verse displays the exposing light, summoning the sleeper to awake (v.14).
14:
"Wherefore he saith, Awake thou that sleepest, and arise from the dead, and Christ shall give thee light." The witnesses note the summons to awake and arise to Christ's light — and the crux of the quotation's source. On "wherefore he saith," interpreters divide over the source. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson, with Clarke, hold this is most likely a fragment of an early Christian hymn (its rhythmical form suggesting a sung verse, like 1 Timothy 3:16). The Reformed Calvin, with Barnes and Wesley, take it as Christ speaking through the gospel, freely alluding to the prophets (especially Isaiah 60:1; 26:19) — not a verbatim citation but the constant message of gospel preaching. Some of the Fathers (Epiphanius, Syncellus) supposed a lost apocryphal source (a prophecy of Elijah or Jeremiah); Grotius and Clarke (alternatively) read "it (the light/gospel) saith." Barnes himself doubts any quotation is intended, taking Paul to speak as an inspired man.

On "Awake thou that sleepest, and arise from the dead," the witnesses note the call. Barnes and Robertson note the summons to the sinner, asleep and dead in sin: "awake (rouse from spiritual slumber) and arise from the dead (the death of sin)" — a call to spiritual life.

On "Christ shall give thee light," the witnesses note the promise. Barnes and Calvin note the promise to the awakened: "Christ shall give thee light (or, shall dawn upon thee)" — Christ, the light, will shine on those who rouse and turn to him; his light awakens and resuscitates the dead. The verse displays the summons to awake and arise to Christ's light.

The witnesses agree the sense: the summons to the sinner asleep and dead in sin — "awake, arise from the dead" — with the promise "Christ shall give thee light (dawn upon thee)," Christ's light awakening and resuscitating the dead (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But the source divides them: Ellicott, Robertson, and Clarke favour an early Christian hymn; Calvin, Barnes, and Wesley take it as Christ speaking through the gospel, freely alluding to Isaiah; some Fathers supposed a lost apocryphal prophecy; Grotius read "it (the light) saith." The verse displays the summons and its crux. The verse marks the source: hymn, Isaiah, or apocryphal. The verse displays the summons: awake and arise. The verse displays the promise: Christ shall give light. The verse displays the call: to spiritual life. The summons displays the dead called to Christ's awakening light. The verse displays the awaking: from sleep. The verse displays the arising: from death. The verse displays the light: Christ's gift. The summons to awake and arise to Christ's light displays the gospel's call to the spiritually dead and a crux of its source: "awake, arise from the dead, and Christ shall give thee light" — read as an early Christian hymn (Ellicott, Robertson), Christ's gospel message alluding to Isaiah (Calvin, Barnes), or a lost prophecy (the Fathers). The verse displays the call to spiritual life, requiring a circumspect walk (v.15).
15:
"See then that ye walk circumspectly, not as fools, but as wise," The witnesses note the call to walk circumspectly and wisely. On "see then that ye walk circumspectly," the grammatical voices note the carefulness. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge: "see (take heed) then that ye walk circumspectly (akribos — carefully, accurately, exactly)" — believers are to order their lives with exact care and watchfulness, attentive to their conduct.

On "not as fools, but as wise," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Chrysostom note the alternative: "not as fools (the unwise, who live carelessly and without thought) but as wise (the prudent, who order life by true wisdom)" — the choice between a foolish and a wise walk.

The puritan Henry notes the diligence: the Christian life requires watchful, careful, wise conduct — not the heedless living of the foolish, but the deliberate prudence of the wise. The verse displays the call to walk circumspectly and wisely.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul charges "see (take heed) that ye walk circumspectly (carefully, exactly)," ordering life with exact care and watchfulness (Barnes, Robertson); "not as fools (careless, thoughtless) but as wise (prudent, ordering life by wisdom)," the choice between foolish and wise walking (Barnes, Chrysostom); the Christian life requiring watchful prudence (Henry). The verse displays the circumspect walk. The verse marks the charge: walk circumspectly. The verse displays the care: exact and watchful. The verse displays the contrast: fools or wise. The verse displays the diligence: required. The call displays believers walking with careful wisdom. The verse displays the circumspection: charged. The verse displays the folly: avoided. The verse displays the wisdom: pursued. The call to walk circumspectly and wisely displays the carefulness the Christian life requires: believers are to take heed and order their conduct with exact care — not as the foolish who live heedlessly, but as the wise who order life by true wisdom. The verse displays the circumspect walk, redeeming the time (v.16). The carefulness here enjoined is not the anxious scrupulosity that frets over trifles, but the steady, watchful exactness of one who knows that he walks through a world full of snares and that every step must be taken with deliberate thought; and the contrast between the fool and the wise is precisely the contrast between the man who lives at random, heedless of consequence, and the man who orders his whole course by the wisdom that comes from above.
16:
"Redeeming the time, because the days are evil." The brief verse notes the call to redeem the time in evil days. The grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note the charge: "redeeming the time (exagorazomenoi ton kairon — buying up the opportunity, making the most of every season)" — believers are to seize and use well every opportunity for good, as a merchant buys up a valuable commodity.

On "redeeming the time," the witnesses note the urgency. Barnes and Calvin note that time and opportunity are precious and fleeting; to "redeem" them is to rescue them from waste and turn them to good account; no opportunity for godliness or service is to be lost.

On "because the days are evil," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the reason: "the days are evil" — full of sin, danger, and temptation, perhaps also of persecution; the evil of the times makes the wise use of every opportunity the more urgent. The verse displays the call to redeem the time in evil days.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge "redeeming the time (buying up the opportunity, making the most of every season)," seizing every opportunity for good (Barnes, Robertson); time and opportunity precious and fleeting, to be rescued from waste and turned to good account (Barnes, Calvin); "because the days are evil," full of sin and danger, making the wise use of time the more urgent (Wesley). The verse displays redeeming the time. The verse marks the charge: redeem the time. The verse displays the figure: buying up opportunity. The verse displays the urgency: precious and fleeting. The verse displays the reason: the days are evil. The call displays believers seizing every opportunity for good. The verse displays the time: redeemed. The verse displays the opportunity: bought up. The verse displays the days: evil. The call to redeem the time in evil days displays the urgency of the wise walk: believers are to buy up every opportunity, making the most of every fleeting season for good — the more urgently because the days are evil, full of sin and danger. The verse displays the redeeming of time, by understanding God's will (v.17).
17:
"Wherefore be ye not unwise, but understanding what the will of the Lord is." The brief verse notes the call to understand the will of the Lord. The grammatical voices note the wisdom. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's repeated charge: "be ye not unwise (foolish, senseless) but understanding (perceiving, grasping) what the will of the Lord is" — believers are to apply themselves to know and comprehend God's will.

On "the will of the Lord," the witnesses note the object. Barnes and Calvin note that true wisdom consists in "understanding what the will of the Lord is" — what God requires and approves; the wise walk is grounded in knowing God's will, not in human cleverness.

The grammatical-historical note the connection: this sums up the call to wisdom (v.15) — to be wise is precisely to understand and do the will of the Lord. The verse displays the call to understand the will of the Lord.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul charges "be ye not unwise (foolish) but understanding (grasping) what the will of the Lord is," applying themselves to know God's will (Barnes, Robertson); true wisdom consisting in "understanding the will of the Lord," what God requires and approves (Barnes, Calvin); this summing up the call to wisdom (grammatical-historical). The verse displays understanding God's will. The verse marks the charge: be not unwise. The verse displays the wisdom: understanding God's will. The verse displays the object: what the Lord requires. The verse displays the connection: true wisdom. The call displays believers grasping the will of the Lord. The verse displays the folly: avoided. The verse displays the understanding: of his will. The verse displays the wisdom: grounded in God. The call to understand the will of the Lord displays the substance of the wise walk: believers are not to be foolish but to apply themselves to know and comprehend the will of the Lord — for true wisdom lies in understanding and doing what God requires. The verse displays the knowing of God's will, fulfilled in being filled with the Spirit (v.18).
18:
"And be not drunk with wine, wherein is excess; but be filled with the Spirit;" The witnesses note the contrast between drunkenness and being filled with the Spirit. On "be not drunk with wine, wherein is excess," the grammatical voices note the prohibition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul forbids drunkenness: "be not drunk with wine, wherein is excess (asotia — riot, profligacy, dissoluteness)" — intoxication, which leads to debauchery and the loss of self-control.

On "but be filled with the Spirit," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Calvin note the striking antithesis: over against being filled with wine stands "be filled with (in, by) the Spirit" — instead of the false stimulation and excess of drink, believers are to be filled with the Holy Spirit, who produces a true and holy exhilaration.

On "be filled with the Spirit," the eastern Cyril and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the command: the filling is a continual, present command — believers are to be ever filled with the Spirit, the source of spiritual joy, song, and thanksgiving (vv.19-20). The verse displays the contrast between drunkenness and being filled with the Spirit.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul forbids "be not drunk with wine, wherein is excess (riot, profligacy)," intoxication leading to debauchery (Barnes, Robertson); the striking antithesis "be filled with the Spirit," over against drink's false stimulation the believer filled with the Holy Spirit, a true holy exhilaration (Barnes, Calvin); the filling a continual command, source of joy and song (Cyril, Wesley). The verse displays the contrast. The verse marks the prohibition: not drunk with wine. The verse displays the excess: riot. The verse displays the antithesis: filled with the Spirit. The verse displays the command: continual filling. The contrast displays the Spirit's fulness replacing drunken excess. The verse displays the wine: forbidden. The verse displays the Spirit: the filling. The verse displays the joy: holy. The contrast between drunkenness and being filled with the Spirit displays the believer's true source of joy: not the riotous excess of wine, with its loss of self-control, but the continual filling of the Holy Spirit — a holy exhilaration that overflows in song and thanksgiving. The verse displays the fulness of the Spirit, expressed in spiritual song (v.19).
19:
"Speaking to yourselves in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord;" The brief verse notes the call to mutual praise in psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs. The grammatical voices note the expression of the Spirit's fulness. Barnes and Robertson note that the fulness of the Spirit overflows in praise: "speaking to yourselves (to one another) in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs" — believers edifying and encouraging each other through sacred song.

On "psalms and hymns and spiritual songs," the witnesses note the variety. Barnes and Calvin note the three kinds: "psalms" (the Old Testament psalms, or songs to instrumental accompaniment), "hymns" (songs of praise to God), and "spiritual songs" (sacred songs of various kinds, prompted by the Spirit) — a rich variety of praise.

On "singing and making melody in your heart to the Lord," the eastern Cyril and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the inwardness and direction: the singing is "in your heart" (from the heart, with inward devotion, not mere lip-service) and "to the Lord" (directed to God, for his glory). The verse displays the call to mutual praise in psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Spirit's fulness overflows in praise — "speaking to yourselves (to one another) in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs," believers edifying each other through sacred song (Barnes, Robertson); the three kinds — psalms (OT), hymns (praise to God), spiritual songs (Spirit-prompted) — a rich variety (Barnes, Calvin); "singing in your heart" (inward devotion) "to the Lord" (for God's glory, Cyril, Wesley). The verse displays mutual praise. The verse marks the song: psalms, hymns, spiritual songs. The verse displays the mutuality: to one another. The verse displays the inwardness: in the heart. The verse displays the direction: to the Lord. The praise displays the Spirit's fulness overflowing in song. The verse displays the psalms: sung. The verse displays the heart: making melody. The verse displays the Lord: the object. The call to mutual praise in psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs displays the overflow of the Spirit's fulness: believers, filled with the Spirit, edify one another through sacred song of every kind — singing and making melody from the heart, directed to the Lord. The verse displays the song of the Spirit-filled, joined to constant thanksgiving (v.20).
20:
"Giving thanks always for all things unto God and the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ;" The brief verse notes the call to constant thanksgiving for all things. The grammatical voices note the scope. Barnes and Robertson note the further mark of the Spirit-filled life: "giving thanks always (at all times) for all things (in every circumstance) unto God and the Father" — a continual, comprehensive gratitude.

On "always for all things," the witnesses note the comprehensiveness. Barnes and Calvin note the breadth: thanksgiving is to be "always" (without ceasing) and "for all things" — not only for blessings but in every circumstance, even adversity, trusting God's good purpose; the believer finds cause for thanks in all.

On "in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ," the eastern Cyril and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the means: thanksgiving is offered "in the name of (through, by the mediation of) our Lord Jesus Christ" — all our praise and thanks reach God through Christ, the one mediator. The verse displays the call to constant thanksgiving for all things.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the mark of the Spirit-filled life — "giving thanks always (at all times) for all things (in every circumstance) unto God and the Father," a continual comprehensive gratitude (Barnes, Robertson); thanksgiving "always" and "for all things," not only for blessings but in adversity, trusting God's purpose (Barnes, Calvin); "in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ," through Christ the mediator (Cyril, Wesley). The verse displays constant thanksgiving. The verse marks the scope: always, for all things. The verse displays the object: God the Father. The verse displays the breadth: in every circumstance. The verse displays the means: in Christ's name. The thanksgiving displays gratitude in all things through Christ. The verse displays the thanks: always. The verse displays the all things: included. The verse displays the name: of Christ. The call to constant thanksgiving for all things displays the grateful spirit of the Spirit-filled: believers are to give thanks at all times and for all things — in every circumstance, even adversity — to God the Father, through the name of the Lord Jesus Christ. The verse displays the thanksgiving of the Spirit-filled, leading to mutual submission (v.21).
21:
"Submitting yourselves one to another in the fear of God." The witnesses note the call to mutual submission in the fear of God. On "submitting yourselves one to another," the grammatical voices note the mutual duty. Barnes and Robertson note the transition to the household duties: "submitting yourselves (yielding, subordinating yourselves) one to another" — a general principle of mutual deference and humility among believers, each yielding to others in love.

On the scope, the witnesses note the breadth and the application. Barnes and Henry note that this mutual submission is the broad principle that governs the specific relationships now to follow (wives and husbands, children and parents, servants and masters); each is to fulfil his proper duty in a spirit of humble submission, though the particular submission differs by relation.

On "in the fear of God," the Reformed Calvin notes the motive: this submission is "in the fear of God (or of Christ)" — not from mere social custom but from reverence for God, who appoints these relations and to whom all are accountable. The verse displays the call to mutual submission in the fear of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "submitting yourselves (yielding) one to another," a general principle of mutual deference and humility, each yielding to others in love (Barnes, Robertson); this the broad principle governing the household duties to follow, each fulfilling his proper duty in humble submission though the particular differs (Barnes, Henry); "in the fear of God," from reverence for God who appoints the relations (Calvin). The verse displays mutual submission. The verse marks the duty: submit one to another. The verse displays the breadth: mutual deference. The verse displays the application: the household duties. The verse displays the motive: the fear of God. The submission displays believers yielding to one another in reverence. The verse displays the submitting: mutual. The verse displays the humility: required. The verse displays the fear: of God. The call to mutual submission in the fear of God displays the principle that governs the Christian household: believers are to yield to one another in humble deference and love — out of reverence for God — a general submission that grounds the particular duties of wives, husbands, children, and servants now to follow. The verse displays the principle of submission, applied first to wives (vv.22-24).
22:
"Wives, submit yourselves unto your own husbands, as unto the Lord." The witnesses note the call for wives to submit to their husbands as to the Lord. On "wives, submit yourselves unto your own husbands," the grammatical voices note the duty. Barnes and Robertson note the first household duty: "wives, submit yourselves (the verb carried over from v.21) unto your own husbands" — the wife is to yield to and honour her own husband's leadership in the home.

On "as unto the Lord," the witnesses note the manner and motive. Barnes and Chrysostom note that the submission is "as unto the Lord" — rendered as part of her devotion to Christ, as though to the Lord himself; her submission to her husband is a form of obedience to Christ, who appointed the order.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the dignity: this is no degrading servitude but the honourable order God established; the wife submits not to a tyrant but to a husband who is to love her as Christ loves the church (v.25). The verse displays the call for wives to submit to their husbands as to the Lord.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the first household duty — "wives, submit yourselves unto your own husbands," the wife yielding to and honouring her husband's leadership (Barnes, Robertson); "as unto the Lord," rendered as part of her devotion to Christ, a form of obedience to him who appointed the order (Barnes, Chrysostom); no degrading servitude but the honourable order God established (Henry, Calvin). The verse displays wifely submission. The verse marks the duty: submit to your husband. The verse displays the manner: as unto the Lord. The verse displays the motive: devotion to Christ. The verse displays the dignity: honourable order. The submission displays the wife yielding as to the Lord. The verse displays the wife: submitting. The verse displays the husband: honoured. The verse displays the Lord: the motive. The call for wives to submit to their husbands as to the Lord displays the first of the household duties: the wife is to yield honour and submission to her own husband — rendered as to the Lord himself, as part of her obedience to Christ, in the honourable order God appointed. The verse displays the wife's submission, grounded in the husband's headship (v.23).
23:
"For the husband is the head of the wife, even as Christ is the head of the church: and he is the saviour of the body." The brief verse notes the husband's headship, patterned on Christ the head of the church. The grammatical voices note the analogy. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of the wife's submission: "the husband is the head of the wife, even as (after the pattern of) Christ is the head of the church" — the husband's headship in the home mirrors Christ's headship over the church.

On "head," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Calvin note that "head" denotes leadership, authority, and care — not tyranny but the responsible direction that a head gives to the body; the husband leads as Christ leads the church.

On "he is the saviour of the body," the eastern Cyril notes the distinctive: of Christ it is uniquely added that "he is the saviour of the body" (the church) — a glory belonging to Christ alone; yet the husband too is, in his measure, the protector and provider for his wife. The verse displays the husband's headship patterned on Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground of submission — "the husband is the head of the wife, even as (after the pattern of) Christ is the head of the church," the husband's headship mirroring Christ's (Barnes, Robertson); "head" denoting leadership, authority, and care, not tyranny but responsible direction (Barnes, Calvin); of Christ uniquely "he is the saviour of the body," a glory belonging to Christ, though the husband too protects his wife (Cyril). The verse displays the husband's headship. The verse marks the headship: of the wife. The verse displays the pattern: Christ and the church. The verse displays the meaning: leadership and care. The verse displays the distinctive: Christ the saviour. The headship displays the husband leading as Christ leads the church. The verse displays the husband: the head. The verse displays the Christ: the head. The verse displays the saviour: of the body. The husband's headship, patterned on Christ the head of the church, displays the ground of the wife's submission: as Christ is the head of the church — and uniquely its saviour — so the husband is the head of the wife, leading and caring for her as the head directs and protects the body. The verse displays the headship of the husband, drawing the analogy of the church's subjection (v.24).
24:
"Therefore as the church is subject unto Christ, so let the wives be to their own husbands in every thing." The brief verse notes the wife's submission patterned on the church's subjection to Christ. The grammatical voices note the analogy applied. Barnes and Robertson note the conclusion ("therefore"): "as the church is subject unto Christ, so let the wives be to their own husbands" — the church's glad subjection to Christ is the model for the wife's submission to her husband.

On "in every thing," the witnesses note the scope. Barnes and Calvin note the breadth: "in every thing" — in all things lawful and proper, within the sphere of the marriage relation; the submission is comprehensive, though always (as understood) limited by obedience to God, never requiring sin.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the harmony: as the church's subjection to Christ is loving and willing, so the wife's submission is to be — not slavish but the glad yielding of love within God's appointed order. The verse displays the wife's submission patterned on the church's subjection to Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the conclusion — "as the church is subject unto Christ, so let the wives be to their own husbands," the church's glad subjection the model (Barnes, Robertson); "in every thing," comprehensive, in all things lawful within the marriage relation, limited by obedience to God (Barnes, Calvin); the submission loving and willing like the church's, not slavish (Wesley). The verse displays the wife's submission. The verse marks the pattern: church subject to Christ. The verse displays the application: wives to husbands. The verse displays the scope: in every thing. The verse displays the limit: lawful and proper. The submission displays the wife yielding as the church to Christ. The verse displays the church: subject. The verse displays the wife: submitting. The verse displays the scope: comprehensive. The wife's submission patterned on the church's subjection to Christ displays the measure of her duty: as the church is gladly and lovingly subject to Christ in all things, so the wife is to submit to her own husband in everything proper — a willing submission within God's appointed order. The verse displays the wife's glad subjection, balanced by the husband's sacrificial love (vv.25-33).
25:
"Husbands, love your wives, even as Christ also loved the church, and gave himself for it;" The witnesses note the call for husbands to love their wives as Christ loved the church. On "husbands, love your wives," the grammatical voices note the duty. Barnes and Robertson note the husband's corresponding duty, weightier than the wife's submission: "husbands, love your wives" — the husband's whole obligation is summed in self-giving love, not mere authority.

On "even as Christ also loved the church," the witnesses note the pattern. Barnes and Calvin note the measure: "even as (after the pattern of) Christ also loved the church" — the husband's love is to be patterned on Christ's love for the church, the highest conceivable standard.

On "and gave himself for it," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the depth: Christ "gave himself for it (the church)" — his love went to the length of dying for her; the husband's love is to be self-sacrificing, ready to give itself wholly for the wife's good. The verse displays the call for husbands to love their wives as Christ loved the church.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the husband's duty — "husbands, love your wives," summed in self-giving love not mere authority (Barnes, Robertson); "even as Christ also loved the church," patterned on Christ's love, the highest standard (Barnes, Calvin); "gave himself for it," Christ's love going to the length of dying, the husband's love self-sacrificing (Cyril, Calvin). The verse displays the husband's love. The verse marks the duty: love your wives. The verse displays the pattern: Christ loved the church. The verse displays the depth: gave himself for it. The verse displays the measure: self-sacrificing. The love displays the husband loving as Christ loved the church. The verse displays the husband: loving. The verse displays the Christ: the pattern. The verse displays the sacrifice: himself. The call for husbands to love their wives as Christ loved the church displays the weighty duty answering the wife's submission: the husband is to love his wife with a self-giving love patterned on Christ, who loved the church and gave himself for her — a love ready to sacrifice all for her good. The verse displays the husband's sacrificial love, with Christ's sanctifying purpose for the church (v.26).
26:
"That he might sanctify and cleanse it with the washing of water by the word," The brief verse notes Christ's purpose to sanctify and cleanse the church. The grammatical voices note the design. Barnes and Robertson note the purpose of Christ's self-giving: "that he might sanctify (set apart, make holy) and cleanse (purify) it (the church)" — Christ gave himself not only to redeem but to purify the church, making her holy.

On "with the washing of water by the word," the witnesses note the means. Barnes and Calvin note the means of cleansing: "the washing of water by the word" — most take this as referring to baptism (the outward washing) joined with the word (the gospel, which gives baptism its meaning and power); the cleansing is by the word applied, of which baptism is the sign.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the inward reality: the outward washing signifies the inward cleansing of the soul by the word and Spirit; the word is the instrument by which Christ purifies his church. The verse displays Christ's purpose to sanctify and cleanse the church.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purpose of Christ's self-giving — "that he might sanctify (make holy) and cleanse (purify) the church," giving himself not only to redeem but to purify her (Barnes, Robertson); the means "the washing of water by the word," most taking it of baptism joined with the word (the gospel) that gives it meaning (Barnes, Calvin); the outward washing signifying inward cleansing by word and Spirit (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Christ sanctifying the church. The verse marks the purpose: sanctify and cleanse. The verse displays the means: washing of water by the word. The verse displays the sign: baptism. The verse displays the reality: inward cleansing. The purpose displays Christ purifying his church by the word. The verse displays the sanctifying: of the church. The verse displays the washing: by the word. The verse displays the cleansing: inward. Christ's purpose to sanctify and cleanse the church displays the aim of his self-giving love: he gave himself that he might make the church holy and pure — cleansing her by the washing of water joined with the word, the outward sign of the inward purifying of the soul. The verse displays the sanctifying of the church, that she might be presented glorious (v.27).
27:
"That he might present it to himself a glorious church, not having spot, or wrinkle, or any such thing; but that it should be holy and without blemish." The witnesses note Christ presenting the church glorious and without blemish. On "present it to himself a glorious church," the grammatical voices note the goal. Barnes and Robertson note the ultimate purpose of Christ's sanctifying work: "that he might present it (the church) to himself a glorious church (in splendour, radiant)" — Christ will at last present the church to himself in perfect glory.

On "not having spot, or wrinkle, or any such thing," the witnesses note the perfection. Barnes and Calvin note the figure of a flawless bride: "not having spot (stain), or wrinkle (mark of age or decay), or any such thing" — the church will be perfectly pure and beautiful, free from every defect.

On "holy and without blemish," the eastern Cyril notes the consummation: the church will be "holy and without blemish (unblemished, faultless)" — the final, perfect holiness of the church, the bride made spotless for her husband Christ; this is the goal of all his sanctifying work. The verse displays Christ presenting the church glorious and without blemish.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ultimate purpose — "that he might present the church to himself a glorious (radiant) church," presented at last in perfect glory (Barnes, Robertson); the figure of a flawless bride, "not having spot (stain) or wrinkle or any such thing," perfectly pure and beautiful (Barnes, Calvin); "holy and without blemish (faultless)," the final perfect holiness of the spotless bride (Cyril). The verse displays the glorious church. The verse marks the goal: present it glorious. The verse displays the perfection: no spot or wrinkle. The verse displays the consummation: holy and without blemish. The verse displays the figure: the spotless bride. The goal displays Christ presenting the church perfectly pure. The verse displays the glory: of the church. The verse displays the spotlessness: complete. The verse displays the holiness: faultless. Christ presenting the church glorious and without blemish displays the consummation of his sanctifying love: he gave himself and purifies the church that he might at last present her to himself in perfect glory — a radiant bride, without spot or wrinkle, holy and faultless. The verse displays the glorious destiny of the church, illustrating the husband's duty (vv.28-29).
28:
"So ought men to love their wives as their own bodies. He that loveth his wife loveth himself." The brief verse notes that men ought to love their wives as their own bodies. The grammatical voices note the application. Barnes and Robertson note the inference ("so"): from Christ's love for the church, "men ought to love their wives as their own bodies" — with the same care a man naturally has for his own body, since husband and wife are one flesh.

On "he that loveth his wife loveth himself," the witnesses note the identity. Barnes and Calvin note the reasoning: "he that loveth his wife loveth himself" — because the two are one, the husband who loves his wife is, in effect, loving himself; her good is his own.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the naturalness: to love one's wife is not a burden but the most natural thing, for she is part of oneself; the husband cares for her as for his own body. The verse displays that men ought to love their wives as their own bodies.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inference — from Christ's love, "men ought to love their wives as their own bodies," with the care a man has for his own body, since the two are one flesh (Barnes, Robertson); "he that loveth his wife loveth himself," the two being one, so loving her is loving himself (Barnes, Calvin); to love one's wife the most natural thing, she being part of oneself (Wesley). The verse displays loving the wife as oneself. The verse marks the application: love wives as own bodies. The verse displays the identity: loving her is loving himself. The verse displays the union: one flesh. The verse displays the naturalness: care for one's own. The love displays the husband loving his wife as his own body. The verse displays the wives: loved as bodies. The verse displays the husband: loving himself. The verse displays the union: of the two. That men ought to love their wives as their own bodies displays the measure and naturalness of the husband's love: as a man cares for his own body, so he ought to love his wife — for the two are one flesh, and he that loves his wife loves himself. The verse displays the husband's love as self-love, grounded in the union of flesh (v.29).
29:
"For no man ever yet hated his own flesh; but nourisheth and cherisheth it, even as the Lord the church:" The brief verse notes the husband nourishing his wife as Christ the church. The grammatical voices note the natural care. Barnes and Robertson note the appeal to nature: "no man ever yet hated his own flesh (his own body), but nourisheth (feeds, sustains) and cherisheth (warms, fosters, tends) it" — every man naturally cares for and tends his own body.

On "nourisheth and cherisheth," the witnesses note the warmth. Barnes and Calvin note the tender terms: to "nourish" is to feed and sustain, to "cherish" is to warm and foster (as a bird warms its young); the husband is to care for his wife with such nourishing tenderness.

On "even as the Lord the church," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the pattern: this is "even as the Lord (Christ) the church" — Christ nourishes and cherishes the church with tender care, and the husband's care for his wife is to mirror it. The verse displays the husband nourishing his wife as Christ the church.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the appeal to nature — "no man ever hated his own flesh, but nourisheth (sustains) and cherisheth (warms, fosters) it," every man caring for his own body (Barnes, Robertson); the tender terms, to nourish is to feed, to cherish is to warm and foster, the husband caring for his wife with such tenderness (Barnes, Calvin); "even as the Lord the church," Christ nourishing the church, the husband's care mirroring it (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays nourishing the wife. The verse marks the nature: none hates his own flesh. The verse displays the care: nourish and cherish. The verse displays the tenderness: warm and foster. The verse displays the pattern: Christ and the church. The care displays the husband tending his wife as Christ the church. The verse displays the flesh: not hated. The verse displays the nourishing: tender. The verse displays the Lord: the pattern. The husband nourishing his wife as Christ the church displays the tender care the husband owes: as no man hates but rather nourishes and cherishes his own body, so the husband is to nourish and cherish his wife — even as Christ tenderly nourishes and cherishes the church. The verse displays the husband's nourishing love, grounded in union with Christ (v.30).
30:
"For we are members of his body, of his flesh, and of his bones." The brief verse notes believers as members of Christ's body. The grammatical voices note the union. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of Christ's care for the church: "we are members of his body" — believers are united to Christ as members to a body, parts of him, sharing his life; this is why he nourishes and cherishes the church.

On "of his flesh, and of his bones," the witnesses note the intimacy. Barnes and Calvin note the strong figure (echoing Genesis 2:23, Adam's words of Eve): "of his flesh, and of his bones" — expressing the closest possible union between Christ and believers, as intimate as that of Adam and Eve, or of body and members.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the wonder: the believer's union with Christ is so close that we are reckoned part of him, his flesh and bones; such is the intimacy of the mystical union. The verse displays believers as members of Christ's body.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground of Christ's care — "we are members of his body," believers united to Christ as members to a body, sharing his life (Barnes, Robertson); "of his flesh, and of his bones" (echoing Genesis 2:23), the closest possible union, as intimate as Adam and Eve or body and members (Barnes, Calvin); the union so close we are reckoned part of him (Wesley). The verse displays union with Christ. The verse marks the membership: of his body. The verse displays the intimacy: his flesh and bones. The verse displays the echo: Genesis 2:23. The verse displays the union: closest possible. The membership displays believers intimately united to Christ. The verse displays the members: of his body. The verse displays the flesh: his. The verse displays the union: intimate. Believers as members of Christ's body display the ground of his nourishing love for the church: as we are members of his body, his flesh and his bones, joined to him in the closest union, so Christ cares for the church as for his own body — and the husband's love for his wife mirrors this. The verse displays the mystical union of Christ and the church, illustrated by the one flesh of marriage (v.31).
31:
"For this cause shall a man leave his father and mother, and shall be joined unto his wife, and they two shall be one flesh." The brief verse notes the one flesh of marriage, cited from Genesis. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Genesis 2:24 (the institution of marriage): "for this cause shall a man leave his father and mother, and shall be joined (cleave, be glued) unto his wife, and they two shall be one flesh" — the foundational statement of the marriage union.

On "one flesh," the witnesses note the union. Barnes and Calvin note that "they two shall be one flesh" expresses the intimate, indissoluble union of husband and wife — no longer two but one; the closest of human bonds, taking precedence even over the tie to parents.

On the application, the grammatical-historical note the typology: Paul cites this not merely about marriage but because it pictures the union of Christ and the church (v.32) — the one flesh of marriage foreshadowing the union of Christ with his people. The verse displays the one flesh of marriage, cited from Genesis.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Genesis 2:24 — "a man shall leave his father and mother, and be joined (cleave) unto his wife, and they two shall be one flesh," the foundational statement of marriage (Barnes, Robertson); "one flesh" expressing the intimate, indissoluble union, no longer two but one, the closest human bond (Barnes, Calvin); cited because it pictures the union of Christ and the church (grammatical-historical). The verse displays one flesh. The verse marks the citation: Genesis 2:24. The verse displays the leaving: father and mother. The verse displays the joining: to the wife. The verse displays the union: one flesh. The citation displays marriage as the closest union, picturing Christ and the church. The verse displays the man: leaving and cleaving. The verse displays the wife: joined. The verse displays the flesh: one. The one flesh of marriage, cited from Genesis, displays the foundation of the marriage union and its deeper meaning: a man leaves father and mother and is joined to his wife, and the two become one flesh — the intimate, indissoluble bond that itself foreshadows the union of Christ and the church. The verse displays the one flesh of marriage, declared a great mystery (v.32).
32:
"This is a great mystery: but I speak concerning Christ and the church." The witnesses note the great mystery of Christ and the church — and the crux of marriage as a sacrament. On "this is a great mystery," interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, with the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Clarke, hold "mystery (mysterion)" means a profound, once-hidden truth now revealed — here the deep union of Christ and the church, of which marriage is a figure; "great" because so profound. They sharply reject the Roman Catholic reading that this proves marriage to be a sacrament, which arose solely from the Vulgate's rendering of mysterion by "sacramentum" — a verbal accident, not the sense of the Greek; Calvin calls it a "blunder" of "the grossest ignorance," Barnes and Clarke that the original conveys no such idea.

On "but I speak concerning Christ and the church," the witnesses note Paul's own explanation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's express disclaimer: "I speak concerning Christ and the church" — he declares that his statement of the mystery refers chiefly (Barnes: solely) to the union of Christ and the church, not to marriage as such; the antitype, not merely the type, is his theme.

The witnesses note the wonder: the spiritual union of Christ and his church is so profound that no language fully explains it. The verse displays the great mystery of Christ and the church.

The witnesses agree the substance: "mystery" is a profound, once-hidden truth — the deep union of Christ and the church, of which marriage is the figure — so great that no language fully explains it; and Paul's own words "I speak concerning Christ and the church" show that the antitype is his chief theme (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). And they agree against the Roman Catholic crux: the doctrine that this proves marriage a sacrament rests only on the Vulgate's "sacramentum" for mysterion, a verbal accident the Greek does not support — rejected by Calvin (a "blunder"), Barnes, and Clarke. The verse displays the mystery and its crux. The verse marks the mystery: Christ and the church. The verse displays the meaning: a profound revealed truth. The verse displays the rejection: marriage as a sacrament. The verse displays the disclaimer: I speak of Christ and the church. The mystery displays the union of Christ and the church, not a sacrament of marriage. The verse displays the mystery: great. The verse displays the union: spiritual. The verse displays the sacrament-reading: rejected. The great mystery of Christ and the church displays a profound truth and a famous crux: the "great mystery" is the deep spiritual union of Christ and his church (figured by marriage), which Paul says is his theme — not, as the Vulgate's "sacramentum" was made to imply, proof that marriage is a sacrament, a reading the Protestant voices reject as a verbal accident. The verse displays the mystery of Christ and the church, closing with the summary duty (v.33).
33:
"Nevertheless let every one of you in particular so love his wife even as himself; and the wife see that she reverence her husband." The witnesses note the summary duty: the husband to love, the wife to reverence. On "let every one of you so love his wife even as himself," the grammatical voices note the husband's duty restated. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's practical summary ("nevertheless," returning from the digression on Christ and the church to the immediate duty): "let every one of you in particular so love his wife even as himself" — each husband to love his own wife as he loves himself.

On "the wife see that she reverence her husband," the witnesses note the wife's duty. Barnes and Calvin note the corresponding charge: "the wife see that she reverence (phobetai — fear, in the sense of respect, honour) her husband" — the wife is to give her husband reverence and honour; the love and the reverence answer to each other.

The eastern Cyril notes the balance: the two duties together — the husband's self-giving love and the wife's reverent honour — make the Christian marriage a reflection of Christ and the church. The verse displays the summary duty: the husband to love, the wife to reverence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's practical summary — "let every one of you in particular so love his wife even as himself," each husband loving his own wife as himself (Barnes, Robertson); "the wife see that she reverence (respect, honour) her husband," giving him reverence and honour (Barnes, Calvin); the two duties together reflecting Christ and the church (Cyril). The verse displays the summary duty. The verse marks the husband: love his wife as himself. The verse displays the wife: reverence her husband. The verse displays the correspondence: love and reverence. The verse displays the reflection: of Christ and the church. The summary displays the husband loving, the wife reverencing. The verse displays the love: of the husband. The verse displays the reverence: of the wife. The verse displays the balance: of duties. The summary duty — the husband to love, the wife to reverence — displays the practical close of the household teaching: each husband is to love his own wife as himself, and the wife to reverence and honour her husband; the self-giving love and the reverent honour together making the marriage a reflection of Christ and his church. The verse displays the mutual duties of marriage, closing the great chapter on love, light, and the household of God.
